You are a teammate on the swarm-wave team. You have two tasks:

**Task #6: [str-28vd.8] Agent bugfix workflow skill** (P2)
Add a project-local Claude agent workflow/skill for bug fixes. Enforces: failing test first, verify failure, implement fix, run pre-completion checks (scripts/quality/pre-completion.sh).

**Task #7: [str-28vd.9] Agent pre-completion workflow** (P2)
Add a project-local Claude agent workflow/skill for session completion. Invokes scripts/quality/pre-completion.sh, completion guidance references the workflow instead of duplicating the checklist.

1. Mark task #6 as in_progress with TaskUpdate
2. `bd start str-28vd.8` to create branch

9. Commit, mark task #7 as completed
10. Send a message to team-lead with results

Note: Separate issues, separate branches.
</teammate-message>

○ str-28vd.9 · Agent pre-completion workflow   [● P2 · OPEN]
Owner: Ketan Gangatirkar · Type: task
Created: 2026-03-11 · Updated: 2026-03-11

DESCRIPTION
Add a project-local Claude/agent workflow definition for session completion that runs the repository-owned pre-completion checks and standardizes what counts as done before an agent reports completion.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
A documented local agent workflow or skill exists for pre-completion; it invokes scripts/quality/pre-completion.sh; completion guidance references the workflow instead of duplicating the check list inline

LABELS: agents, ci, docs, tooling

PARENT
  ↑ ○ str-28vd: (EPIC) Epic: Process guardrails rollout ● P1

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→```
    13→
    14→## Issue Title Guidelines
    15→
    16→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    17→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    18→
    19→| Bad | Good |
    20→|---|---|
    21→| `Add support for symbolic integers` | `Symbolic int support` |
    22→| `Implement the protocol message for branch data` | `Branch data protocol msg` |
    23→| `Fix bug where path constraints are dropped` | `Path constraints dropped` |
    24→| `Create new frontend handler for Go` | `Go frontend handler` |
    25→
    26→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Protocol …", "Solver …", "CLI …"), not filler verbs
    27→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    28→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    29→
    30→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
    31→
    32→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
    33→
    34→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
    35→
    36→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
    37→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
    38→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
    39→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
    40→   ```bash
    41→   git pull --rebase
    42→   git push
    43→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
    44→   ```
    45→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
    46→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
    47→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
    48→
    49→**CRITICAL RULES:**
    50→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
    51→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
    52→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
    53→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
    54→
    55→## Creating Issues
    56→
    57→### Issue Shape: Prefer End-to-End Value
    58→
    59→When breaking down a plan, default to **vertical slices**, not layer slices.
    60→Each workable issue should deliver **one complete, testable unit of value**,
    61→even if that unit is very small.
    62→
    63→**The key test:** after merging this issue alone, can a user, teammate, or
    64→agent actually do something new end-to-end? If not, the issue is probably cut
    65→at the wrong seam.
    66→
    67→**Good issue shape:**
    68→- A thin end-to-end slice through the system for one narrow scenario
    69→- May touch multiple layers (storage, API, CLI/UI) if that is what makes the
    70→  scenario actually usable
    71→- Produces a behavior that can be demonstrated, tested, and described in one
    72→  sentence of user-facing value
    73→
    74→**Bad issue shape:**
    75→- One issue for database changes, one for API wiring, one for frontend wiring
    76→  when none of them is useful alone
    77→- Pure plumbing issues for a feature that only become valuable after several
    78→  sibling issues land
    79→- "First backend, then frontend, then integration" breakdowns for user-facing
    80→  work
    81→
    82→**Default rule:** if the work is a `feature`, split by **user journey, use case,
    83→or behavior**, not by architecture layer.
    84→
    85→**Examples:**
    86→
    87→| Layer-sliced breakdown | End-to-end breakdown |
    88→|---|---|
    89→| `Add DB schema for saved filters` + `Add saved filters API` + `Build saved filters UI` | `Create a saved filter`, `Rename a saved filter`, `Delete a saved filter` |
    90→| `Branch data Rust types` + `Branch data TS handler` + `Branch data Go handler` | `Scan shows branch source locations`, `Explore reports branch conditions`, `Export includes branch metadata` |
    91→| `Add parser support` + `Add core model` + `Add CLI flag` | `CLI accepts one new config option and applies it during scan` |
    92→
    93→**Exception:** layer- or infrastructure-shaped issues are acceptable only when
    94→there is genuinely no smaller end-to-end slice worth landing yet. Those should
    95→usually be `task` or narrowly scoped enabling work, not the default shape for a
    96→feature plan.
    97→
    98→### Issue Sizing: Keep Issues Small
    99→
   100→Every issue should represent **one focused, independently completable unit of work**.
   101→A well-sized issue can be implemented, tested, and merged in a single session.
   102→
   103→**Target size:** 1–3 files changed, under ~200 lines of diff. If an issue looks
   104→bigger, break it down.
   105→
   106→**How to tell an issue is too big:**
   107→- The description has multiple distinct deliverables or bullet points
   108→- You find yourself thinking "first I need to X, then Y, then Z" — each of those is its own issue
   109→- The acceptance criteria have more than 3 items
   110→- It bundles multiple user journeys or behaviors into one issue
   111→- The issue only delivers partial plumbing and depends on sibling issues before
   112→  any value appears
   113→
   114→**What to do instead:**
   115→- Create an **epic** for the larger goal, then create small child issues under it
   116→- Each child issue should be one **end-to-end behavior**: "create one saved
   117→  filter", "scan one new file pattern", "export one new metadata field"
   118→- Use `--parent` and `--waits-for` to link children to the epic
   119→- Use `bd dep add` for ordering constraints between children
   120→
   121→**Examples:**
   122→
   123→| Too big | Break into |
   124→|---|---|
   125→| `Add symbolic integer support` | Epic + `Int equality branches work end-to-end`, `Int comparisons solve end-to-end`, `Int arithmetic flows through explore` |
   126→| `Protocol message for branch data` | `Explore reports branch source lines`, `Spec JSON includes branch metadata`, `Scan forwards branch metadata to reports` |
   127→| `Fix and test path constraint bug` | `Repro test for path constraints`, `Fix path constraint drop` |
   128→
   129→**Rule of thumb:** If you can't describe the issue in one sentence without "and",
   130→it's two issues.
   131→
   132→**Second rule of thumb:** If you can only describe the issue as a component
   133→handoff like "add backend support" or "wire the frontend", it is probably not
   134→an end-to-end issue yet.
   135→
   136→**Always specify `--type` explicitly** — never rely on the default. Use the correct issue type:
   137→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability (most issues are features)
   138→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
   139→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
   140→- `task` — narrow infrastructure chore with no user-visible effect (CI config, project init, doc-only changes). If it adds capability, it's a `feature`.
   141→
   142→### Creating Epics
   143→
   144→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
   145→
   146→```bash
   147→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
   148→  -d "Description of the epic" \
   149→  --waits-for-gate all-children
   150→```
   151→
   152→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
   153→
   154→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
   155→
   156→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
   157→
   158→```bash
   159→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l core,rust \
   160→  --parent str-abc \
   161→  --waits-for str-abc \
   162→  -d "Description" \
   163→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
   164→```
   165→
   166→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for str-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
   167→
   168→### Dependencies Between Issues
   169→
   170→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
   171→
   172→```bash
   173→bd dep add str-child str-dependency   # str-child is blocked by str-dependency
   174→```
   175→
   176→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the executor cannot be built before the instrumentor exists"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
   177→
   178→### Bug Fix Policy: Reproduce Before Fixing
   179→
   180→All bugs must be reproduced in a good automated test **before** attempting any fix.
   181→The workflow is:
   182→
   183→1. Write a test that demonstrates the bug (the test must **fail**)
   184→2. Verify the test fails for the right reason
   185→3. Implement the fix
   186→4. Verify the test now **passes**
   187→
   188→This ensures every bug fix is validated by a regression test and prevents
   189→"fixes" that don't actually address the root cause.
   190→
   191→### Completing an Issue
   192→
   193→An issue is not complete until:
   194→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, clippy/lint, build)
   195→2. Changes are committed on a **dedicated branch for this issue only** (no other issues' changes)
   196→3. The branch is merged to `main` (one branch at a time — do not merge other branches simultaneously)
   197→4. The branch is deleted after merge
   198→5. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   199→6. Changes are pushed to remote with `git push`
   200→
   201→Do not leave stale branches. Merge and delete promptly.
   202→
   203→**Never** combine work for multiple issues on one branch. If you discover
   204→additional work while on an issue branch, create a new issue and report it to
   205→the team lead (or note it for later) — do NOT implement it yourself. Address
   206→it on a separate branch after merging the current one. Scope creep across
   207→agents is the primary cause of duplicate commits and orphan branches.
   208→
   209→## Git Workflow
   210→
   211→- Work on feature branches, not `main` directly
   212→- **One branch per issue** — never combine multiple issues in a single branch
   213→- Branch names should reference the issue: `str-<hash>-short-description`
   214→- Commits should be clean and atomic — one logical change per commit
   215→- Rebase feature branches onto `main` before merging
   216→- **Merge one branch at a time** — merge branch A to `main`, push, then merge branch B. Never batch-merge multiple branches in a single operation.
   217→- Merge to `main` directly — do NOT create pull requests
   218→- After merge, delete the feature branch both locally and remotely
   219→- Work is complete when changes are on `main` and pushed, not when a branch is pushed
   220→- **Never cherry-pick commits.** Cherry-picking creates duplicate commits with
   221→  different SHAs, making history confusing and leaving orphan branches that
   222→  appear unmerged. Always merge or rebase entire branches instead.
   223→
   224→### Commit Early, Commit Often
   225→
   226→Sessions can be interrupted at any time — context window compaction, usage limits,
   227→crashes, or network failures. **Uncommitted work is lost work.**
   228→
   229→- **Commit after every meaningful change.** Don't accumulate multiple logical
   230→  changes before committing. If you've made something work — tests pass, a
   231→  function is implemented, a bug is fixed — commit it immediately.
   232→- **Push your branch frequently.** A local commit is better than uncommitted
   233→  changes, but a pushed commit survives everything. Push after every 1–2 commits.
   234→- **Never hold work "until it's ready."** A series of small, incremental commits
   235→  on a feature branch is vastly better than one big commit that might never land.
   236→- **Teammates in worktrees**: push your branch before reporting task completion.
   237→  If the session is interrupted before the lead merges your branch, pushed work
   238→  can be recovered. Unpushed work in a worktree cannot.
   239→
   240→### Commit messages and issue references
   241→
   242→When working on a beads issue, **prefix the commit message with the issue key**.
   243→This links commits to the issue they address and makes history easy to trace.
   244→
   245→Format: `<issue-key>: <description>`
   246→
   247→Example for feature commits:
   248→```
   249→str-a1b: Add symbolic integer support to concolic engine
   250→
   251→Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
   252→path constraint generation for branch conditions.
   253→```
   254→
   255→When a commit addresses multiple issues, use the primary issue as the prefix
   256→and list others in the body:
   257→```
   258→str-a1b: Add symbolic integer support to concolic engine
   259→
   260→Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
   261→path constraint generation for branch conditions.
   262→
   263→Also: str-c2d, str-e3f
   264→```
   265→
   266→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files) without a
   267→specific feature issue, list all affected issue IDs in the body:
   268→```
   269→bd sync: close str-a1b, str-c2d
   270→
   271→Issues: str-a1b, str-c2d
   272→```
   273→
   274→### Parallel work with agent teams (preferred)
   275→
   276→For parallel work on multiple issues, use Claude Code's **agent teams** instead
   277→of manually running separate Claude instances in different worktrees. Teams
   278→coordinate through the Task tool and each teammate runs in an isolated worktree
   279→automatically (via `isolation: "worktree"` on the Task tool).
   280→
   281→**Why teams over manual worktrees:**
   282→- Claude manages concurrency — no Dolt database conflicts
   283→- Teammates coordinate via task lists and messages
   284→- Worktree lifecycle is fully automatic
   285→- The team lead merges results back to `main`
   286→
   287→**Supervision:** Always spawn teammates with `mode: "plan"`. Each teammate
   288→must write a plan and get approval from the team lead before implementing.
   289→This ensures the user can review proposed approaches before code is written.
   290→
   291→**Quick start:** Use `/swarm` to automate the full parallel workflow — triage,
   292→team setup, plan review, merge, quality gates, and close protocol.
   293→
   294→**Manual workflow (if not using `/swarm`):**
   295→```
   296→1. Create tasks for each issue (TaskCreate)
   297→2. Spawn teammates with isolation: "worktree", mode: "plan" (Task tool)
   298→3. Each teammate works on exactly ONE issue on its own branch
   299→4. Each teammate researches and proposes a plan
   300→5. Team lead reviews and approves/rejects each plan
   301→6. Approved teammates implement, team lead merges results ONE AT A TIME
   302→   (merge branch A → main → push, then merge branch B → main → push, etc.)
   303→```
   304→
   305→**Preventing duplicate work across agents:**
   306→- The team lead must assign each issue to exactly one teammate. Never assign
   307→  the same issue to multiple agents.
   308→- Teammates must check `bd show <id>` before starting — if the issue is already
   309→  `in_progress` or `closed`, stop and ask the lead for a different assignment.
   310→- The team lead must mark issues `in_progress` (with assignee) before spawning
   311→  the teammate, not after.
   312→
   313→**Post-swarm cleanup:**
   314→After all teammates finish and their branches are merged, the team lead must:
   315→1. Delete all merged worktree branches: `git branch -d <branch>`
   316→2. Verify no orphan branches remain: `git branch --no-merged main`
   317→3. Clean up worktree directories if any remain under `.claude/worktrees/`
   318→
   319→Orphan branches from interrupted swarms create confusion — they appear unmerged
   320→but contain duplicate work that already landed on `main` via a different branch.
   321→
   322→### Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
   323→
   324→Agents MUST run **`/pre-completion`** before announcing work is done — whether
   325→reporting to a team lead or to the user. This skill verifies quality gates, E2E
   326→tests, commits pushed, scope, and acceptance criteria. Do not declare completion
   327→until `/pre-completion` reports PASS.
   328→
   329→The `/pre-completion` output table is proof of completion. Agents MUST include
   330→it verbatim in their completion message. Team leads MUST reject completion
   331→announcements that lack the table or show any FAIL status.
   332→
   333→This is a hard requirement, not a suggestion. Agents that skip `/pre-completion`
   334→risk announcing "done" with failing tests, unpushed commits, or scope creep —
   335→all of which have caused regressions in this project.
   336→
   337→### Worktree isolation for teammates
   338→
   339→When spawned as a teammate with `isolation: "worktree"`, you run in a separate
   340→copy of the repo. **Violating isolation corrupts other agents' work.**
   341→
   342→**Rules:**
   343→- **Never `cd` to the main repo**. Stay in your worktree directory (under
   344→  `.claude/worktrees/`). If unsure, run `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.
   345→- **Never `git checkout`** to switch branches. You are already on your branch.
   346→- **Never run git commands with `-C` pointing to the main repo**. All git
   347→  operations must target your worktree.
   348→- **Verify isolation** before critical operations: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`
   349→  must return a path containing `.claude/worktrees/`.
   350→- **Commit and push only your branch**. The team lead merges to main.
   351→
   352→**Why this matters:** All worktrees share the same `.git` directory. Running
   353→`git checkout` in the main repo changes HEAD for the main working directory,
   354→which other agents and the team lead depend on. This causes branch switching
   355→races, lost commits, and merge failures.
   356→
   357→### Worktree workflow (single-agent)
   358→
   359→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   360→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   361→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   362→
   363→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   364→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   365→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   366→
   367→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   368→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   369→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   370→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   371→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   372→and cause confusing errors.
   373→
   374→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   375→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   376→
   377→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   378→
   379→### Why bd?
   380→
   381→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   382→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   383→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   384→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   385→
   386→### Quick Start
   387→
   388→**Check for ready work:**
   389→
   390→```bash
   391→bd ready --json
   392→```
   393→
   394→**Create new issues:**
   395→
   396→```bash
   397→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t feature -p 0-4 --json  # ALWAYS specify -t
   398→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -t bug -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   399→```
   400→
   401→**Claim and update:**
   402→
   403→```bash
   404→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   405→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   406→```
   407→
   408→**Complete work:**
   409→
   410→```bash
   411→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   412→```
   413→
   414→### Issue Types
   415→
   416→- `bug` - Something broken
   417→- `feature` - New functionality
   418→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   419→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   420→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   421→
   422→### Priorities
   423→
   424→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   425→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   426→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   427→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   428→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   429→
   430→### Workflow for AI Agents
   431→
   432→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   433→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   434→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   435→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   436→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   437→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   438→
   439→### Auto-Sync
   440→
   441→bd automatically syncs issue data to git:

1→---
     2→name: pre-completion
     3→description: Verify all completion criteria before declaring work done. Runs quality gates, E2E tests, checks walkthrough requirements, verifies commits are pushed, and confirms no scope creep.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Pre-Completion: Done Criteria Verification
     8→
     9→Verify all completion criteria are met before announcing work is done to the
    10→team lead (if in a team) or the user (if working solo).
    11→**Do not declare completion until this skill passes.**
    12→
    13→**If any check fails, you are NOT done.** Fix the failing issue, then re-run
    14→`/pre-completion` from the top. Repeat until all checks pass. Do not announce
    15→completion, send a completion message, or report to the lead with any FAIL
    16→status — go back and fix it first.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## Phase 1 — Determine What Changed
    21→
    22→1. **Identify changed files**:
    23→   ```bash
    24→   git diff --name-only main...HEAD
    25→   ```
    26→   If no commits yet vs main, use `git diff --name-only HEAD` for staged/unstaged changes.
    27→
    28→2. **Classify changes** into categories:
    29→   - `rust-core` — files in `shatter-core/`
    30→   - `rust-cli` — files in `shatter-cli/`
    31→   - `typescript` — files in `shatter-ts/`
    32→   - `go` — files in `shatter-go/`
    33→   - `rust-frontend` — files in `shatter-rust/`
    34→   - `protocol` — any `protocol.rs` or `protocol.ts` or `protocol/` files
    35→   - `pipeline` — solver, instrumentor, explorer, orchestrator, or CLI wiring
    36→   - `cli-output` — CLI commands, formatting, example files, demo scripts
    37→   - `other` — docs, config, examples
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## Phase 2 — Quality Gates
    42→
    43→Run the appropriate language-specific gates based on what changed, plus E2E tests unconditionally.
    44→
    45→### Language-specific (if the language was touched):
    46→
    47→1. **Rust** (if `rust-core` or `rust-cli` changed):
    48→   ```bash
    49→   cargo test
    50→   cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    51→   ```
    52→
    53→2. **TypeScript** (if `typescript` changed):
    54→   ```bash
    55→   cd shatter-ts && npm test
    56→   cd shatter-ts && npx tsc --noEmit
    57→   ```
    58→
    59→3. **Go** (if `go` changed):
    60→   ```bash
    61→   cd shatter-go && go test ./...
    62→   cd shatter-go && go vet ./...
    63→   ```
    64→
    65→4. **Rust frontend** (if `rust-frontend` changed):
    66→   ```bash
    67→   cd shatter-rust && cargo test
    68→   cd shatter-rust && cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    69→   ```
    70→
    71→### Always required:
    72→
    73→5. **E2E concolic tests** — always run regardless of what changed:
    74→   ```bash
    75→   cargo test --test e2e_concolic
    76→   ```
    77→   These are the only tests that validate the full pipeline end-to-end. A module
    78→   can pass all its own tests while being silently disconnected from the pipeline.
    79→
    80→### Conditional:
    81→
    82→6. **Protocol sync** (if `protocol` changed):
    83→   - Verify protocol types are consistent across all languages.
    84→   - Run cross-language tests (Full tier).
    85→
    86→7. **Walkthrough** (if `cli-output` changed):
    87→   ```bash
    88→   bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0
    89→   ```
    90→   Errors in steps 1-19 indicate regressions — fix before proceeding.
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Phase 3 — Code Quality Checks
    95→
    96→1. **No magic numbers/string literals**: Verify constants are defined, not inline literals.
    97→2. **No `unwrap()` in library code** (Rust): Verify `Result` and `?` are used.
    98→3. **No `any` type** (TypeScript): Verify proper typing throughout.
    99→4. **Parallel code path parity**: If the change touches one of a parallel pair
   100→   (e.g., `buildSymExpr` / `buildSymExprWithFlow`, random explorer / concolic
   101→   orchestrator, CLI wiring for `--concolic` vs default), verify the other path
   102→   handles the same cases.
   103→5. **Bug fix has reproduction test**: If this is a bug fix, verify a test exists
   104→   that would fail without the fix.
   105→
   106→---
   107→
   108→## Phase 4 — Git Status
   109→
   110→1. **All changes committed**:
   111→   ```bash
   112→   git status --porcelain
   113→   ```
   114→   - Working tree must be clean (no uncommitted changes).
   115→   - If there are uncommitted changes, commit them first.
   116→
   117→2. **Branch pushed** (if in a worktree or on a feature branch):
   118→   ```bash
   119→   git log --oneline origin/<branch>..HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "no upstream"
   120→   ```
   121→   - All commits must be pushed. If unpushed commits exist, push now:
   122→     ```bash
   123→     git push -u origin HEAD
   124→     ```
   125→
   126→3. **Commit messages reference the issue**:
   127→   - Verify commit messages follow `<issue-key>: <description>` format.
   128→
   129→---
   130→
   131→## Phase 5 — Scope Verification
   132→
   133→1. **No scope creep**: Compare the changed files and functionality against the
   134→   original issue description.
   135→   - If you implemented something not in the issue, create a new beads issue for it
   136→     and either revert or note the scope expansion.
   137→   - Extra work discovered during implementation should be filed as new issues,
   138→     not silently included.
   139→
   140→2. **Issue acceptance criteria met**: Re-read the issue description and verify
   141→   every acceptance criterion is satisfied.
   142→
   143→---
   144→
   145→## Output
   146→
   147→Report a summary table. **This table IS the proof of completion.** You MUST
   148→include it verbatim in your completion message to the team lead (via
   149→`SendMessage`) or in your final response to the user. The lead will reject
   150→completion announcements that do not include this table.
   151→
   152→```
   153→| Check                        | Status      | Notes                   |
   154→|------------------------------|-------------|-------------------------|
   155→| Rust tests                   | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   156→| Rust clippy                  | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   157→| TypeScript tests             | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   158→| TypeScript types             | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   159→| Go tests                     | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   160→| Go vet                       | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   161→| Protocol sync                | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   162→| E2E concolic                 | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   163→| Walkthrough                  | PASS / FAIL / N/A | ...              |
   164→| Parallel path parity         | PASS / N/A  | ...                     |
   165→| Bug repro test exists        | PASS / N/A  | ...                     |
   166→| All changes committed        | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   167→| Branch pushed                | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   168→| Commit messages valid        | PASS / WARN | ...                     |
   169→| Scope matches issue          | PASS / WARN | ...                     |
   170→| Acceptance criteria met      | PASS / FAIL | ...                     |
   171→
   172→Pre-completion: PASS / FAIL
   173→```
   174→
   175→If any required check is FAIL, set overall status to **FAIL** and list what must
   176→be fixed. **Do not stop here** — go fix the failures, then re-run `/pre-completion`.
   177→You must keep iterating until the table shows `Pre-completion: PASS`. Only then
   178→may you send a completion message.
   179→

1→---
     2→name: swarm
     3→description: Work all ready beads issues in parallel using agent teams with worktree isolation. Triages issues, spawns teammates, reviews plans, merges results, and runs quality gates.
     4→user-invocable: true
     5→---
     6→
     7→# Swarm: Parallel Issue Work
     8→
     9→Work `bd ready` issues in parallel using agent teams. Each teammate works in an
    10→isolated worktree with plan-mode supervision.
    11→
    12→## Usage
    13→
    14→- `/swarm` — work all `bd ready` issues (max 8 concurrent teammates)
    15→- `/swarm 3` — work `bd ready` issues, max 3 concurrent teammates
    16→- `/swarm 3 id-abc id-def id-ghi` — specific issues, max 3 concurrent
    17→
    18→## Batch Size Limit
    19→
    20→A single swarm run processes **at most 20 tasks**. Managing more than 20 tasks
    21→in one context window degrades quality. If the total eligible issue count exceeds
    22→20, the swarm works the first 20 (by priority), then hands off the remainder
    23→via the continuation protocol below.
    24→
    25→---
    26→
    27→## Phase 0 — Discover Project Config
    28→
    29→Look for `.claude/swarm-config.md` in the project root. If it exists, read it
    30→and use its values for the phases below. It may define any of:
    31→
    32→- **Quality gate commands** — what to run before declaring work complete
    33→- **Pre-completion skill** — a project skill teammates must run before reporting done
    34→- **Post-merge hooks** — commands to run on main after merging a branch
    35→- **Branch naming** — convention for worktree branch names (default: `<issue-id>`)
    36→
    37→If the file does not exist, use these defaults:
    38→- Quality gates: whatever the project's CLAUDE.md describes for running tests/lints
    39→- Pre-completion: none (teammates just report done with quality gate results)
    40→- Post-merge hooks: none
    41→- Branch naming: `<issue-id>`
    42→
    43→---
    44→
    45→## Phase 1 — Triage
    46→
    47→**Parse arguments**: The first argument, if numeric, is the max concurrent teammates.
    48→Remaining arguments are issue IDs. If no numeric first argument, concurrency defaults to 8.
    49→
    50→**Check for continuation file**: If `.claude/swarm-continue.txt` exists and no
    51→issue IDs were passed as arguments, read the IDs and concurrency from the file,
    52→delete it, and use those IDs as the input set (skip `bd ready`).
    53→
    54→1. Run `bd ready` (or use the provided issue IDs, or the continuation file)
    55→2. **Skip epics** — epics are containers, not units of work. Filter out any issue
    56→   with `[epic]` in the title or `type: epic`.
    57→3. Run `bd show <id>` for each non-epic issue to understand scope
    58→4. Identify issues that touch **overlapping files** — these must be sequenced, not parallelized
    59→5. Skip issues that are too large or ambiguous for a single teammate (flag for manual work)
    60→6. **Apply batch limit**: If eligible issues exceed **20**, take the top 20 by
    61→   priority (P0 first, then dependency order). The remainder become the
    62→   **overflow set** — they will be worked in a subsequent `/swarm` invocation
    63→   after this batch completes (see Phase 7).
    64→7. Of the issues in this batch (≤20), if they exceed the concurrency cap
    65→   (default 8), prioritize by beads priority and dependency order. Remaining
    66→   issues go into a **wait queue** and will be spawned as active teammates
    67→   complete (see Phase 4 — Backfill on Completion).
    68→8. Present the triage plan to the user:
    69→   - Which issues will be parallelized (batch 1)
    70→   - Which issues are in the wait queue (if limited by concurrency)
    71→   - Which issues overflow to the next batch (if limited by the 20-task cap)
    72→   - Which issues are sequenced and why
    73→   - Which issues are skipped and why
    74→
    75→**Wait for user approval before proceeding.**
    76→
    77→---
    78→
    79→## Phase 2 — Team Setup
    80→
    81→1. Create a team via `TeamCreate` (name: `swarm-YYYY-MM-DD` or similar)
    82→2. Create a task (via `TaskCreate`) for each parallelizable issue, including the
    83→   beads ID in the task description
    84→3. For each task, spawn a teammate via the `Agent` tool with:
    85→   - `subagent_type: "general-purpose"`
    86→   - `mode: "plan"`
    87→   - `team_name: <team>`
    88→   - Do NOT use `isolation: "worktree"` — it is unreliable. Instead, include
    89→     manual worktree setup commands in the prompt (see below).
    90→4. Each teammate's prompt must include:
    91→   - The full issue details (from `bd show`)
    92→   - Instruction to follow CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md conventions
    93→   - The quality gate commands from swarm-config (or CLAUDE.md)
    94→   - If swarm-config defines a pre-completion skill, instruction to run it and
    95→     include the output in the completion message
    96→   - **Manual worktree setup** (copy verbatim into each prompt, substituting
    97→     the branch name per the naming convention):
    98→     ```
    99→     ## WORKTREE SETUP — RUN FIRST
   100→     Before doing anything else, create and enter an isolated worktree:
   101→     ```bash
   102→     REPO_ROOT="$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)"
   103→     BRANCH_NAME="<branch>"
   104→     WORKTREE_DIR="$REPO_ROOT/.claude/worktrees/$BRANCH_NAME"
   105→
   106→     if [ -d "$WORKTREE_DIR" ]; then
   107→       echo "Worktree already exists, reusing"
   108→     else
   109→       git worktree add "$WORKTREE_DIR" -b "$BRANCH_NAME"
   110→     fi
   111→
   112→     cd "$WORKTREE_DIR"
   113→     ```
   114→     Verify you are in the right place:
   115→     ```bash
   116→     echo "CWD: $(pwd)"
   117→     echo "Branch: $(git branch --show-current)"
   118→     echo "$(pwd)" | grep -q '\.claude/worktrees/' && echo "WORKTREE VERIFIED" || echo "FATAL: NOT in a worktree"
   119→     ```
   120→     If verification fails, STOP and report the error to the team lead.
   121→
   122→     ALL subsequent commands must run from this worktree directory.
   123→     NEVER cd back to the main repo directory. Commit and push ONLY on
   124→     your worktree branch — never touch main.
   125→
   126→     When done, push your branch:
   127→     ```bash
   128→     git push -u origin HEAD
   129→     ```
   130→     ```
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## Phase 3 — Plan Review
   135→
   136→For each teammate that submits a plan:
   137→
   138→1. Review the plan for:
   139→   - Correct scope (matches the issue, no scope creep)
   140→   - Quality gates included in the plan
   141→   - Test coverage plan (tests are comprehensive and well-targeted)
   142→   - No overlap with other teammates' work
   143→   - Working directory references `.claude/worktrees/` (not the main repo)
   144→2. Approve or reject via `plan_approval_response`
   145→3. If rejecting, provide specific feedback on what to change
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→## Phase 4 — Monitor & Merge
   150→
   151→As teammates complete work:
   152→
   153→1. **Verify quality**: Confirm the teammate ran the required quality gates and
   154→   they passed. If a pre-completion skill is configured, confirm the teammate
   155→   included its output and it shows PASS. Reject any completion that doesn't
   156→   meet these criteria — send the teammate back to fix.
   157→2. **Get the branch**: The branch name follows the naming convention from Phase 2
   158→3. **Merge to main**:
   159→   ```bash
   160→   git checkout main
   161→   git merge <branch> --no-edit
   162→   ```
   163→4. **Handle conflicts**: If merge conflicts arise, resolve them or ask the teammate to rebase
   164→5. **Post-merge hooks**: If swarm-config defines post-merge commands, run them now
   165→6. **Close the beads issue**: `bd close <id>`
   166→7. **Clean up worktree and branch**:
   167→   ```bash
   168→   git worktree remove .claude/worktrees/<branch>
   169→   git branch -d <branch>
   170→   ```
   171→8. **Only then** send `shutdown_request` to the teammate
   172→
   173→**CRITICAL**: Never shut down a teammate before merging their branch. Always
   174→merge first, then clean up, then shut down.
   175→
   176→### Backfill on Completion
   177→
   178→Each time a teammate completes and is merged+shut down, if the number of
   179→active teammates is below the concurrency cap and issues remain in the wait
   180→queue:
   181→
   182→1. Re-evaluate the wait queue: pick the highest-priority issue (P0 first,
   183→   then beads priority order) whose files do **not** overlap with any
   184→   currently in-flight teammate's work.
   185→2. If a non-conflicting issue is found, spawn a new teammate for it
   186→   (following Phase 2 setup).
   187→3. If **all** remaining wait-queue issues conflict with in-flight work,
   188→   do **not** spawn — wait until more teammates finish and re-evaluate.
   189→4. Repeat until the wait queue is empty or all remaining issues conflict.
   190→
   191→---
   192→
   193→## Phase 5 — Quality Gates
   194→
   195→After **all** teammates are merged:
   196→
   197→1. Run the project's full quality gate on main (per swarm-config or CLAUDE.md)
   198→2. If any failures, fix them on main directly
   199→3. If swarm-config defines additional post-merge validation (e.g., a review skill
   200→   or integration check), run it now
   201→
   202→---
   203→
   204→## Phase 6 — Close Protocol
   205→
   206→1. Close any remaining beads issues: `bd close <id1> <id2> ...`
   207→2. Sync and push:
   208→   ```bash
   209→   bd sync
   210→   git pull --rebase
   211→   git push
   212→   git status  # Must show "up to date with origin"
   213→   ```
   214→3. Shut down all teammates (if not already done)
   215→4. Delete the team via `TeamDelete`
   216→5. Report summary to user:
   217→   - Issues completed
   218→   - Issues skipped or deferred
   219→   - Any quality gate findings
   220→   - Final git status
   221→6. **Continuation**: If issues remain from the batch overflow (see Phase 1),
   222→   proceed to Phase 7.
   223→
   224→---
   225→
   226→## Phase 7 — Batch Continuation
   227→
   228→If more than 20 eligible issues were identified in Phase 1, only the first 20
   229→are worked in Phases 2–6. After Phase 6 completes:
   230→
   231→1. **Save remaining issue IDs** to `.claude/swarm-continue.txt` (one ID per line)
   232→2. **Report to the user**:
   233→   ```
   234→   Batch 1 complete: <N> issues done, <M> issues remaining.
   235→   Remaining issues saved to .claude/swarm-continue.txt.
   236→   Invoking /swarm to continue with next batch...
   237→   ```
   238→3. **Invoke `/swarm`** with the remaining issue IDs (read from the continue file).
   239→   This starts a fresh context window. The new invocation will:
   240→   - Read `.claude/swarm-continue.txt` if it exists and no explicit issue IDs
   241→     are provided
   242→   - Delete the file after reading it
   243→   - Work up to the next 20 issues, repeating until all are done
   244→
   245→### Continue File Format
   246→
   247→`.claude/swarm-continue.txt`:
   248→```
   249→# Swarm continuation — remaining issues from batch started YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM
   250→# Concurrency: <N>
   251→id-aaa
   252→id-bbb
   253→id-ccc
   254→```
   255→
   256→Phase 1 must check for this file at startup. If it exists and no issue IDs were
   257→passed as arguments:
   258→1. Read the issue IDs and concurrency from the file
   259→2. Delete the file
   260→3. Use those IDs as the input set (skip the `bd ready` call)
   261→
